coredata.dc:title: Fabrication of carbon dots-embedded luminescent transparent wood with ultraviolet blocking and thermal insulating capacities towards smart window application
coredata.dc:creator: Zhou, Ningyu
Long, Shoufu
Song, Dongsheng
Hui, Bin
Cui, Xinjie
An, Congcong
Zhang, Ming
coredata.dc:description: To expand functions of transparent wood (TW) including fluorescence, ultraviolet blocking, heat preservation and insulation, we adopted carbon quantum dots (CQDs) to prepare luminescent transparent wood. CQDs with yellow/red fluorescence (YCD/RCD) were prepared by chitosan and o-phenylenediamine. Afterwards, Balsa woods were pretreated to obtain wood frameworks (DW/LW), which were further combined with epoxy resin for achieving transparent woods (DW-TW/LW-TW). Results showed LW retained more lignin, the LW-TW blocked more ultraviolet light, displaying the better visible transmission and mechanical strength than DW-TW. After adding YCD and RCD to LW-TW, the yellow and red fluorescence transparent woods with outstanding mechanical and ultraviolet blocking properties were prepared, especially the red fluorescence transparent wood (RTW). Specifically, the tensile strength and elongation at break of RTW reached up to 19.39 MPa and 5.35 %, respectively. Moreover, RTW could block 78.8 % of UV-B light and 78 % of UV-A light, respectively. Besides, RTW possessed excellent visible transmission (70.3 %) and UV blocking (88.87 %). Significantly, both RTW and YTW displayed outstanding water repellency, excellent durability, good thermal stability and insulation. Predictably, luminescent transparent woods certainly will enhance the adaptability of wood, and broaden its applications in green decoration, lighting setup, sensor and other fields.

head.ce:title: Fabrication of carbon dots-embedded luminescent transparent wood with ultraviolet blocking and thermal insulating capacities towards smart window application

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: To expand functions of transparent wood (TW) including fluorescence, ultraviolet blocking, heat preservation and insulation, we adopted carbon quantum dots (CQDs) to prepare luminescent transparent wood. CQDs with yellow/red fluorescence (YCD/RCD) were prepared by chitosan and o-phenylenediamine. Afterwards, Balsa woods were pretreated to obtain wood frameworks (DW/LW), which were further combined with epoxy resin for achieving transparent woods (DW-TW/LW-TW). Results showed LW retained more lignin, the LW-TW blocked more ultraviolet light, displaying the better visible transmission and mechanical strength than DW-TW. After adding YCD and RCD to LW-TW, the yellow and red fluorescence transparent woods with outstanding mechanical and ultraviolet blocking properties were prepared, especially the red fluorescence transparent wood (RTW). Specifically, the tensile strength and elongation at break of RTW reached up to 19.39 MPa and 5.35 %, respectively. Moreover, RTW could block 78.8 % of UV-B light and 78 % of UV-A light, respectively. Besides, RTW possessed excellent visible transmission (70.3 %) and UV blocking (88.87 %). Significantly, both RTW and YTW displayed outstanding water repellency, excellent durability, good thermal stability and insulation. Predictably, luminescent transparent woods certainly will enhance the adaptability of wood, and broaden its applications in green decoration, lighting setup, sensor and other fields.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: With the increasing demands towards environmental protection and sustainable development, the importance of renewable biomass resource has been recognized. Wood is a renewable biomass material with clear structural hierarchy, complex and orderly structure, and natural porous characteristics, which is widely used in the fields such as decoration, furniture, architecture, etc. Glazier-based materials have been utilized in windows for the transmittance of light, while their brittleness may result in safety problems. In recent years, transparent wood (TW) products draw considerable attentions as energy-efficient substrates due to their good mechanical properties, low thermal conductivity, and good optical transmittance [1]. The preparing strategy of transparent wood was initially proposed by Fink [2], as the innovative technology for achieving energy-saving material, which not only made full use of wood, but also endowed wood with new function or even intelligence.

Photo-responsive luminescent characteristic is a very important feature for smart materials, which have been applied to various scenarios like traffic clothing and signs [3,4]. Therefore, researches come to focused on the functionalization of transparent wood through the introduction of luminescent materials. The luminescent transparent wood could be applied to traffic signs, safety bar codes, ultraviolet blocking, military camouflage, smart window, ultraviolet conversion, outdoor lighting and many other fields [5]. Meantime, combination with transparent wood could effectively avoid the fluorescence quenching caused by the aggregation of luminescent materials, and the reduction of light stability caused by the long-term exposure. Gan et al. [6] prepared the luminescent and magnetic transparent wood by encapsulating the delignified wood framework with polymethyl methacrylate resin (PMMA) and magnetic γ-Fe2O3@YVO4:Eu3+ nanoparticles, which has great application potential in green LED lighting equipment, luminous magnetic switch and anti-counterfeiting facilities. After immobilizing epoxy resin and rare-earth doped aluminate (REDA) phosphor nanoparticles in lignin-modified wood, El-Naggar et al. [7] developed a translucent wood with long-lasting phosphorescence, high optical transmittance, high photostability and durability, ultraviolet protection and superhydrophobicity. Al-Qahtani et al. [5] permeated lignin-modified wooden substrate with the pre-polymerized methyl methacrylate and photoluminescent lanthanide-doped aluminum strontium oxide (SrAl2O4:Eu2+, Dy3+; ASOED) pigment for fabricating a photoluminescent translucent wood, which offered a substantial color shift from colorless under visible light to green under UV irradiation.

The preparation process of transparent wood mainly includes the structural design of wood framework and the resin impregnation with matching refractive index. The wood delignification is an effective strategy for the structural design of wood framework, which involves the nucleophilic reactions (i.e., chemical pulping) based on Na2SO3 or Na2S systems, or the electrophilic, free radical and oxidation reactions (i.e., chemical bleaching) based on H2O2, NaClO2 (ClO2) or NaClO systems [8]. Such procedure certainly will deconstruct, split and promote the hemicellulose/lignin removal of wood, but should maintain the integrity and hierarchical structure of wood cell wall. Fink initially used NaClO to remove the lignin and other colored substances [2]. Berglund's group obtained the wood framework after NaClO2 treatment, the specific surface area of which increased from 1.2 m2·g−1 (original wood) to 20 m2·g−1 [9]. Research group of Prof. Hu boiled the wood in NaOH and Na2SO3 solution, and then treated with H2O2 solution, resulting in the retained lignin of wood below 3 % [10]. However, complete removal of lignin will seriously reduce the wood structure and strength. Therefore, it is necessary to consider the treating procedure and retained lignin content for the performance and structure design of wood framework. Li et al. proved that complete removal of lignin was not a necessary process for preparing transparent wood [11]. The wood treated with alkaline H2O2 showed the same whiteness as delignified wood framework, retained better mechanical property and more lignin content, displaying less harmful to environment. Apparently, partial delignification gives wood more opportunities for advanced and sustainable applications.

In order to obtain high transmittance transparent wood, the refractive index (RI) of polymer should match with the components in cell wall, especially the main component of cellulose (RI: 1.53) in delignified wood framework. Except for refractive index, the resin properties such as viscosity, compatibility with cell walls, and shrinkage during polymerization have significant impact on the transparent wood performances including optical stability and mechanical property. Li et al. [12] fabricated the transparent wood based on wood framework and PMMA resin (RI: 1.49) by acetylation to improve their compatibility, and eliminate the gap due to their interfacial debonding, thus achieving the high light transmittance of 92 %. Epoxy resin (EP, RI: 1.50) is a series of thermosetting polymers with epoxy groups, which can be cured by heating with curing agent. The polar groups (e.g., hydroxyl, ether bond, ester bond) or non-polar groups (e.g., aromatic ring, alkane) can be introduced to EP as required. Such EP exhibits the excellent mechanical properties, low shrinkage and degradation resistance caused by weather or solvent [13]. These merits make EP a common polymer for preparing transparent wood. Interestingly, the cellulose nanofibers in wood (delignified wood framework) can improve the overall performance of cured EP. Zheng et al. [14] prepared a new EP with multiple functions by changing its network structure, which was impregnated into wood framework to achieve the functional wood with fluorescence that could respond to light, heat and pH.

Carbon quantum dots (CQDs) are the new luminescent materials with excellent biocompatibility, high chemical stability, polychromatic optical property, low toxicity, easy modification, good water solubility and environmental friendliness, which have been widely used in chemical and biological sensing, electrocatalysis, biological imaging, photocatalysis, drug transport, ultraviolet conversion and other fields [15–17]. However, the strong π-π stacking or resonance energy among the solid CQDs will result in the fluorescence quenching, which can be prevented by keeping sufficient space between fluorophores through dispersed media for weakening their interactions [18,19]. Embedding CQDs into transparent wood can eliminate the fluorescence quenching caused by their aggregation effectively. This is because delignified wood framework mainly composed of cellulose can provide the dense hydrogen bonding sites and reduce the non-radiative transitions, which will supply convenience for uniform dispersion and adsorption of CQDs. Besides, the introduction of CQDs not only protects the transparent wood from ultraviolet radiation, but also endows the transparent wood with polychromatic fluorescent and other more functions, thereby enhancing the performance of transparent wood. Balsa wood is able to become an ideal wood framework (cellulose skeleton) after treatment for preparing transparent wood. In this study, the CQDs with yellow (YCD) and red (RCD) fluorescence were prepared by using the chitosan and o-phenylenediamine as the carbon sources. Afterwards, Balsa woods were respectively pretreated by sodium chlorite and hydrogen peroxide to obtained the wood frameworks (delignified wood and lignin-retained wood), which were further combined with epoxy resin and CQDs for achieving transparent woods and luminescent transparent woods. Then the retained lignin content of DW and LW as well as the visible transmission, mechanical strength and ultraviolet blocking property of DW-TW, LW-TW, YTW and RTW were investigated in depth. Besides, the water repellency, durability, thermal stability and insulation of RTW and YTW were also discussed. Predictably, such luminescent transparent woods certainly will enhance the adaptability of wood-based material, and greatly broaden its applications in green decoration, lighting setup, sensor and other fields.

Tangential veneers of Balsa wood (30 mm × 30 mm × 1 mm) were obtained from local factory (Jilin Province, China). Epoxy resin (EP, E51) was purchased from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co. Ltd. Chitosan (CS, Mw = 300,000 g·mol−1) and glacial acetic acid (CH3COOH, 99.5 wt%) were obtained from Shanghai Maclin Biochemical Technology Co., Ltd. Sodium hydroxide (NaOH, 96 wt%), hydrogen peroxide (H2O2, 30 wt%), ethanol (99.5 wt%), sodium chlorite (NaClO2, 80 wt%) and phosphoric acid (H2PO4, 98 wt%) were purchased from Tianjin Damao Chemical Reagent Factory. O-phenylenediamine (98.5 wt%), magnesium sulfate (MgSO4, 99 wt%), ethylene diamine tetraacetic acid disodium (Na2EDTA, 99 wt%) and sulfuric acid (H2SO4, 98 wt%) were purchased from Tianjin Zhiyuan Chemical Reagent Co. Ltd. All reagents in experiment were used as received without further purification.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of CQDs with yellow and red fluorescence

ce:section.ce:para: 0.2 g of CS was evenly dispersed in 20 mL of H2PO4 (20 wt%) solution under stirring and ultrasonic processing for 3–5 min, which were then transferred to a Teflon lined stainless steel reactor and heated to 180 °C for 6 h. After cooling to room temperature, the generated liquid was filtered through a microporous membrane (0.22 μm) to remove the insoluble substances and larger particles. After purification in pure water with a dialysis bag (MW = 100 Da) for 24 h, the CQD solution with yellow fluorescence was freeze-dried, and the as-obtained powders (YCDs) were stored in a dryer.

108 mg of o-phenylenediamine was dissolved in 10 mL of H2SO4 (20 wt%) solution under stirring condition, which were then transferred to a Teflon lined stainless steel reactor and heated to 200 °C for 5 h. After cooling to room temperature, the generated liquid was filtered through a microporous membrane (0.22 μm) to remove the insoluble substances and larger particles. After purification in pure water with a dialysis bag (MW = 100 Da) for 48 h, the CQD solution with red fluorescence was freeze-dried, and the as-obtained powders (RCDs) were stored in a dryer.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Pretreatment of wood with different methods
ce:section.ce:para: As shown in Fig. 1 , natural wood (thickness: 1 mm) was obtained by rotary cutting the Balsa wood. Natural wood was immersed in an aqueous solution with NaOH (3 wt%) and Na2EDTA (0.1 wt%), which together were heated at 70 °C for 10 min. Subsequently, MgSO4 (0.1 wt%) and H2O2 (4.0 wt%) were added, which together were further heated for 6 h. Then the as-prepared white lignin-retained wood (LW) was further placed in deionized water to remove residual chemicals. As a contrast, natural wood was also immersed to the NaClO2 solution (2 wt%, which was adjusted by CH3COOH to keep the pH of mixed solution to 4.6) at the temperature of 80 °C until it turned white completely (~6 h). Finally, the delignified wood (DW) in traditional method was obtained. Notably, DW and LW after water washing were then stored in a mixture of absolute ethanol and acetone for more than 1 h to remove the residual water. Their lignin contents (acid insoluble) were determined in TAPPI method (see Supplementary material).

As shown in Fig. 1, the YCD and RCD were evenly and respectively dispersed to absolute ethanol to acquire YCD solution and RCD solution (10 mg/mL). Then lignin-retained wood was immersed to YCD or RCD solution at room temperature in vacuum (0.05 MPa) for 30 min. Afterwards, EP and curing agent were mixed with the mass ratio of 1:3 evenly, which were then poured into a silicone mold containing the lignin-retained wood impregnated with YCD or RCD. Finally, the transparent wood samples with yellow luminescent (YTW) and red luminescent (RTW) were respectively obtained after treatment at room temperature in vacuum (0.05 MPa) and at the temperature of 80 °C in an oven. As a contrast, EP and curing agent were also poured into the silicone molds containing delignified wood (DW) and lignin-retained wood (LW) without loading CQDs for achieving two types of transparent wood (TW) samples, which were named DW-TW and LW-TW, respectively.

Transmission electron microscope (JEM2100, JEOL, Japan) was used to analyzed the YCD and RCD at an accelerating voltage of 200 kV. Morphologies of natural wood, lignin-retained wood, lignin-retained wood, lignin-retained woods loading with YCD and RCD, YCD and RTW were characterized by field emission environmental scanning electron microscopy (FE-ESEM, Quanta FEG 250). Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FT-IR, Magna-IR 560, Nicolet) and X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS, Thermofisher Scientific Company, K-Alpha) analyzed the chemical constituents of the as-prepared samples. Size distributions of YCD and RCD were measured by a particle size analyzer (Microtrac, Wave II) in deionized water (volume fraction of YCD or RCD was 0.4 %) after ultrasonic treatment for 15 min to minimize the sample aggregates. Excitation and emission spectra of YCD and RCD were measured with a fluorescence spectrophotometer (Cary Eclipse, USA) at ambient temperature. Thermogravimetric analysis (TGA) was performed with a thermogravimetric analyzer (STD Q600, CTA instruments) equipped with a crucible. The samples were heated in nitrogen from 25 °C to 750 °C at a rate of 20 °C/min. Wetting property of sample was carried out by utilizing a contact angle measuring instrument (Powreach, JC2000D), and the final contact angle (CA) was determined by averaging measurements taken from at least five positions on each sample surface. In addition, the evaluations of optical property, mechanical and durability, thermal stability and insulation on wood samples have been also carried out as presented in Supplementary material.

TEM images in Figs. 2a & 3a showed that both YCD and RCD had the excellent dispersion and spherical nanomorphology. As presented in Figs. 2b & 3b, the average particles sizes of YCD and RCD were around 3.71 nm and 12.52 nm, respectively. In the inserted TEM images with high resolution, the lattice fringe spacing of YCD and RCD could be clearly observed with the average distances of 0.21 nm and 0.24 nm, respectively, which was very close to the (100) diffraction plane of graphene. It demonstrated that the as-prepared CQDs (YCD and RCD) had the carbon nuclei mainly consisting of sp2 hybridization, which was similar to the graphite structure [20]. Under the excitation of ultraviolet light, YCD and RCD exhibited the yellow and pink fluorescence, respectively. Their redshifts of emission wavelengths might be attributed to the reduction of the band gap caused by the electron delocalization. Increasing size and heteroatom doping concentration of CQDs would affect such reduction [21].

The functional groups on YCD surface as well as CS were carefully investigated by FTIR spectra as presented in Fig. 2c. The absorption peak of YCD at 1502 cm−1 stem from the stretching vibration of CC in aromatic conjugates, meanwhile, another absorption peak appeared at 1021 cm−1 due to the out-of-plane bending vibration of aromatic CH [22], indicating that YCD was composed of a polycyclic aromatic skeleton. In addition, the absorption peaks at 1400 cm−1 and 1290 cm−1 corresponded to the NH and CN stretching vibrations, respectively [23]. Since YCD was prepared from CS, the peaks near 3440 cm−1 were attributed to the stretching vibrations of OH and NH. Besides, the characteristic absorption peaks at 2877 cm−1 and 1625 cm−1 resulted from the CH in long carbon chain and the acetylated amino group were also observed in the FTIR spectrum of YCD with the partial redshift, indicating that the CS has been reacted with H3PO4 indeed.

The elemental composition of YCD was further analyzed by using X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) as provided in Fig. 2d, which included C, O, N and P. The element P, N, O and C contents of YCD have been provided in Table 1 . Specifically, O 1s spectrum in Fig. 2e could be fitted into three peaks corresponding to PO/C-OH (531.9 eV), COC (531.2 eV) and CO/OCO (530.3 eV), respectively [24]. As the acidic solvent and dopant, H3PO4 could promote the rupture of carbon chain, and involve in the reaction between the N and YCD surfaces. The appearance of PO/C-OH peak was due to the incomplete crystal structure caused by the heteroatom doping [25]. The N 1s spectrum in Fig. 2f could be fitted into four peaks corresponding to pyridine nitrogen (CNC, 397.8 eV), pyrrole nitrogen (CNC, 398.3 eV), graphite nitrogen (NC3, 399.5 eV) and nitrogen oxide (CNO, 401.4 eV), respectively [26]. Such atomic energy formed the higher electron density, which greatly affected the fluorescence of CQDs [27]. The doped H3PO4 allowed the graphitic nitrogen replace some C atoms of CQDs and provided the off-domain electrons, which improved the radiative process and reduced the non-radiative process in aromatic domain, leading to the emission peak redshift of CQDs [28]. The C 1s spectrum in Fig. 2g could be fitted into three peaks corresponding to CC/CC (283.1 eV), CN (284.3 eV) and CO (285.3 eV), respectively [18]. The presence of N and O confirmed the existence of CN and CO on the surface of YCD, which directly affects its surface defect state [29]. From high-resolution P 2p spectrum in Fig. 2h, it could be observed that the peak representing PO/PO was near 132 eV, demonstrating that P doping in the carbon skeleton forms large number of P-containing groups on the surface of CQDs.

In contrast, the absorption peaks around 3390 cm−1 associated with the stretching vibrations of OH and NH were presented in Fig. 3c, and the stretching vibrations of CH in aromatic and alkyl rings were observed around 3110 cm−1 [30]. The absorption peaks at 1500 cm−1 and 1625 cm−1 were attributed to the stretching vibrations of benzene ring skeleton and SH, respectively, which corresponded to o-phenylenediamine, suggesting the presence of the groups containing N, O and S in RCD [31]. In addition, the absorption peak around 2874 cm−1 represented the stretching vibration of CO/CN, while the absorption peak at 1400 cm−1 was associated with the stretching vibration of CN and the deformation vibration of OH [32]. Except for the above groups related to the S element, the stretching vibrations of the SO and CS could also be observed near 1060 cm−1 and 586 cm−1. Based on the above results, it could be seen that the good water solubility of RCD come from the hydrophilic functional groups containing N and S on its surface, and the retained corresponding groups of o-phenylenediamine.

From XPS full scan spectrum in Fig. 3d, RCD consisted of C, O, N, and S. Two different peaks at 532 eV and 533 eV in the O 1s spectrum (Fig. 3e) were attributed to CO and C-OH/COC, respectively [33]. The N 1s spectrum in Fig. 3f could be fitted into three different peaks corresponding to pyridine nitrogen (400.5 eV), pyrrole nitrogen (402.1 eV) and graphite nitrogen (403.1 eV) [34]. The C 1s spectrum (Fig. 3g) could be divided into three different forms, which were attributed to CC/CC (284.8 eV), CN/CO (286.4 eV) and CS/CO/CN (291.8 eV), respectively [35]. Furthermore, the spectrum of S 2p (Fig. 3h) could be divided into two peaks at 168.9 eV and 170.1 eV, which were associated with C-SO2 and C-SO3, respectively [36]. The element S, O, C and N contents of RCD have been provided in Table 1. Above results confirmed that o-phenylenediamine after dehydration and carbonization could form RCD with carbon skeleton embedding of functional groups containing N, S and O.

We recorded the difference of surface color of delignified wood (DW) and lignin-retained wood (LW) with the extension of treating time. Natural wood (NW) appeared brown due to the presence of lignin and light-absorbing chromophores. As shown in Fig. S1a (see Supplementary material), delignified wood gradually turned dark white during the treatment for 6 h, while lignin-retained wood turned white just after the treatment for 1 h. The color parameters (L*, b* and a*) of the wood template samples in Fig. S1b–d showed that the luminosities of DW and LW presented the same trend during the treatment of initial 2 h. Afterwards, the luminosity value of lignin-retained wood reached its maximum at 3 h, then gradually decreased, but was always larger than natural wood. In contrast, the luminosity value of delignified wood reached its maximum at 1 h and finally was close to natural wood after fluctuations. In addition, the b* and a* values of both DW and LW showed the decreasing trend, which had a certain relationship with the lignin content in wood samples. Since lignin content decreased largely in the initial 1 h of treatment, both L* values of DW and LW increased obviously, both a* values of DW and LW dramatically decreased and reached their minimums at 1 h, and both b* values of DW and LW reduced gradually. Differently, L* and a* values of lignin-retained wood were always larger than delignified wood, while b* values of lignin-retained wood were always smaller than delignified wood. Therefore, LW with DW after 3 h of treatment as well as natural wood were selected for ultraviolet blocking evaluation. The ultraviolet blocking rates and the mark images of the ultraviolet light successively passing through the three types of samples on indicator card were shown in Fig. S1e. This is because the lignin molecule contains multiple benzene rings and conjugated double bonds, such structure can absorb the light in UV-B (280–320 nm) and UV-A (320–400 nm) regions simultaneously, thereby protecting wood from ultraviolet damage. Therefore, natural wood presented a UV365nm blocking capacity of 100 %, while DW lost most of its lignin displayed a reduced UV365nm blocking performance of 98.2 %. Notably, lignin-retained wood presented a better UV365nm blocking performance (99.7 %) than that of delignified wood. In conclusion, selecting lignin-retained wood as the wood template for preparing transparent wood could better meet our requirement for UV-blocking.

Table S1 in Supplementary material showed the lignin contents and removal rates of LW and DW after different processing times. Along with the extended time of treatment, the lignin content decreased, and the lignin removal rate increased. Notably, the ClO2 would be generated during the delignification of wood with NaClO2, which preferentially attacks the unsaturated structure of lignin (such as the aromatic ring and alkene side chain), leading to the degradation and conversion of lignin to small molecule products. Moreover, ClO2 could also break the bond between the aromatic ring and side chain in the benzyl alcohol structure to form the lignin fragments for the degradation of lignin [8]. As shown in Table S1, the lignin removal rate of DW dramatically reached up to 63.7 % within 2 h, meanwhile, the lignin content of DW sharply reduced from 29.3 % to 10.6 % within 2 h, and then slowly stabilized to 6.7 % after 6 h. However, delignification rate of DW in initial 2 h was too fast to be regulated.

In contrast, the lignin removal speed of LW was identically 6–7.5 %·h−1 in the first 4 h, and the lignin removal rate of LW reached 58.9 % after 6 h. Meanwhile, its reduced speed of lignin content was 1.8–2 %·h−1 in the first 4 h, and the lignin content finally retained 12.0 % after 6 h. Apparently, more lignin could be retained in the wood sample in H2O2 delignification method. More importantly, its lignin removal speed was more identically, demonstrating the better reaction stability of such process with H2O2, which was more controllable and qualified for obtaining wood framework. This is mainly because the main reaction ion (HOO−) could be easily generated from H2O2 molecule, and break the bond between the aromatic ring and side chain of lignin, as well as the unsaturated bond in the side chain of lignin. The remaining active anions and free radicals (e.g., superoxide, hydroxyl radical, etc.) contribute to the oxidation, hydrolysis and degradation of lignin, leading to the fragmentation of lignin macromolecule and the deconstruction of lignin conjugate system, which could adjust the wood structure at the micro/nano scale [8]. Besides, the chemicals used in the H2O2 reaction process were more environmentally friendly and of lower cost. Therein, the additions of MgSO4 and Na2EDTA respectively served as the chelator and stabilizer to inhibit the decomposition of H2O2 [8] for improving the delignification rate and efficiency, which could achieve a win-win situation in terms of reducing production cost and environmental pollution. In addition, the relevant studies also showed that H2O2 method could make the wood framework (lignin-retained wood) reserve its strength and gloss better [37].

Fig. 4a shows the FTIR spectra of NW, DW, LW, EP and LW-TW. From the FTIR spectrum of natural wood, the absorption peaks at 3440 cm−1, 2920 cm−1 and 1049 cm−1 corresponded to the stretching vibrations of –OH, –CH2 and CO, respectively, meanwhile, the absorption peaks near 1740 cm−1 and 1250 cm−1 corresponded to the stretching vibration of CO in hemicellulose (xylan/glucomannan), the glucuronic acid group of hemicellulose, or the ester bond between lignin and hemicellulose. The absorption peaks at 1610 cm−1 and 1430 cm−1 were attributed to the stretching vibrations of aromatic ring in lignin [9]. In the FTIR spectra of DW and LW, the absorption peaks at 2920 cm−1 and 1740 cm−1 disappeared, meanwhile, the absorption peaks at 1610 cm−1, 1430 cm−1, 1250 cm−1 and 1049 cm−1 representing the main components of wood cell wall weakened. It indicated that the hemicellulose and partial lignin of DW and LW have been removed successfully. However, the absorption peaks around 1250–1000 cm−1 in the spectrum of DW almost disappeared, but still existed in the spectrum of LW, which strongly supported the view that more lignin was retained in LW. EP (E51) contained epoxy group and benzene ring. In the spectrum of EP, the CH stretching vibrations of methyl and methylene were found at 2965 cm−1 and 2865 cm−1, while the absorption peaks around 1610 cm−1 and 830 cm−1 were attributed to the stretching and bending vibrations of epoxy group. Besides, the absorption peaks around 1510 cm−1, 1455 cm−1 and 745 cm−1 corresponded to the stretching vibration of CC, the bending vibration of CH and the bending vibration of CH3 on the benzene ring, respectively [38]. In addition, the FTIR spectrum of LW-TW presented the characteristic absorption peak of lignin-retained wood and EP simultaneously.

According to the FTIR spectra of YTW and RTW provided in Fig. 4b, we could observe the absorption peaks around 2850–2980 cm−1 due to the stretching vibrations of CH in methyl and methylene, and the characteristic peaks of epoxy group at 1610 cm−1 and 830 cm−1, respectively [38]. In addition, the absorption peaks attributed to the stretching vibration of CC (benzene ring), and the bending vibrations of CH and the CH3 also appear around 1510 cm−1, 1455 cm−1 and 745 cm−1, respectively, meanwhile, the characteristic absorption peaks stemming from the main components of wood cell wall also appeared to 1610 m−1, 1430 m−1, 1250 m−1 and 1049 m−1, testifying the existence of EP and wood template (lignin-retained wood). In the FTIR spectrum of YTW, we observed the absorption peaks at 1502 cm−1, 1290 cm−1 and 1021 cm−1, which stem from the stretching vibrations of CC, CH and CN in aromatic compounds [22], indicating the presence of YCD in YTW. Similarly, the FTIR spectrum of RTW displayed the absorption peaks at 586 cm−1, 1500 cm−1 and 1625 cm−1 were attributed to the stretching vibrations of CS, CC/CC (benzene ring) and SH, respectively, demonstrating the presence of RCD in RTW.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Luminescent properties of CQDs and wood samples
ce:section.ce:para: The optical properties of YCD and RCD were evaluated on the basis of their UV–Vis absorption spectra and fluorescence (FL) emission spectra. As inserted in Fig. 2a, as-obtained YCD appeared yellowish in daylight, and emitted yellow fluorescence under an UV365nm lamp. The absorption peak at 260 nm in the UV–Vis absorption spectrum (Fig. 5a) was caused by the n-π* transition of CN/CO, which could be associated with CP, PN and other phosphorous groups by doping phosphoric acid [20]. This is because the addition of phosphoric acid could promote the formation of CN/CO groups [29], which would increase the surface defect density of CQDs to capture more excitons. Meanwhile, the rapid recombination of such excitons would lead to the redshift of emission wavelength towards CQDs (YCD and RCD) [39]. In addition, YCD showed an obvious trend of double emission peaks. At its maximum excitation wavelength of 410 nm, the double emission peaks of YCD appeared, and their centers were located around 480 nm and 600 nm, respectively. However, their intensities were different, and presented yellow fluorescence eventually. With the increasing excitation wavelength in Fig. 5b, except for the emission peak at 480 nm, a new emission peak appeared around 600 nm. However, their centers of emission wavelength hardly changed, showing the excellent excitation-independence of CQDs, which may be related to the large number of defect states on their surfaces [14]. In contrast, RCD showed the brown color in daylight (inserted in Fig. 3a), and emitted the red fluorescence under ultraviolet light (inserted in Fig. 5c). The two characteristic absorption peaks at 280 nm and 612 nm in the UV–Vis absorption spectrum (Fig. 5c) could be attributed to electron transitions of CC and CN/CO [40,41]. In addition, the FL emission spectra of RCD presented the strongest emission peak at 620 nm when its excitation wavelength was 560 nm in Fig. 5d. Notably, the absorption peak of RCD would not change its location with the variation of excitation wavelength, but change its intensity only, fully displaying the outstanding excitation-independence of RCD once again.

From fluorescence microscope image in Fig. 5e1, when natural wood (NW) was irradiated under blue light, a strong yellow fluorescence could be observed, which comes from the lignin. After delignification, its yellow fluorescence became very dim (see Fig. 5f1), proving the successful lignin removal of DW. For lignin-retained wood (LW) under blue light, its fluorescence intensity was much lower than natural wood, but lightly higher than delignified wood as presented in Fig. 5g1. This is because most retained lignin in LW has been modified to remove its chromophores. After adding YCD to lignin-retained wood (YCD-LW), a stronger fluorescence could be observed in Fig. 5h1, which indicated that blue light could excite the YCD, which has successfully adhered to the lignin-retained wood. For lignin-retained wood containing RCD (RCD-LW) under blue light, its fluorescence color was reddish (Fig. 5i1). This is because the excitation light of RCD is near to the blue light, and RCD has been successfully attached to the lignin-retained wood. Then these wood samples were further observed under green light. As provided in Fig. 5e2–g2, the natural wood emitted the strongest red fluorescence, while the fluorescent intensities of DW and LW were fairly low due to the successful delignification and lignin-modification treatments, which was similar to their observations under blue light. For YCD-LW, its fluorescence intensity was dim, but slightly stronger than lignin-retained wood (Fig. 5h2). This is because green light cannot excite the YCD. Interestingly, the fluorescence intensity of RCD-LW was significantly stronger than YCD-LW as shown in Fig. 5i2. It indicated that the excitation light of RCD adhering to the lignin-retained wood was near to the green light. Notably, the fluorescence microscope images of YCD-LW and RCD-LW (Fig. 5i1 & i2) were obtained after orderly rising the lignin-retained woods containing YCD and RCD by 50, 75, 85, 95 vol% ethanol water mixture and absolute ethanol for totally 50 min. Such strong fluorescence of YCD-LW and RCD-LW demonstrated that the CQDs (YCD and RCD) indeed had combined with lignin-retained wood solidly.

As presented in Fig. 6a, natural wood has abundant pores, mainly including the honeycomb cavity (with the diameter of 5–70 μm), the tracheid (with the diameter of 150–200 μm, primary fluid transport channel), and the dense cell wall (composed of cellulose, hemicellulose and lignin). Such three components are closely combined with each other to provide necessary support for the mechanical integrity of wood. The opaque property of natural wood is attributed to the absorption of most light by lignin, as well as the light scattering caused by the mismatch of refractive indexes between the cell wall (cellulose, hemicellulose and lignin) and the air in cell cavity [42]. With the removal of lignin, the cell walls of wood become thinner, some of which even rupture (Fig. 6b). Meanwhile, the wood sample retaining modified lignin (removing the chromophores) maintain its cell walls structure even better (Fig. 6c). As presented in Table S2 and Fig. S2 (see Supplementary material), the density, porosity and BET specific surface area of lignin-retained wood are 85.7 mg/cm3, 99.7 % and 3.25 m2/g, respectively, which are similar but more proper than the pores structure of original wood (92 mg/cm3, 93.8 % and 3.30 m2/g). This is attributed to the significant modification and retention of lignin in LW.

Therefore, lignin-retained wood (LW) was selected to be the ideal wood framework for preparing transparent wood. Although most honeycomb structure of wood was retained, the overall structure of LW turned looser. Meantime, a large number of micropores were generated on the cell wall of LW (main component is cellulose), offering the more channels for the further impregnation of EP (with matched refractive index). Moreover, the chromophore and lignin with strong light absorption have been removed from the wood, giving the as-obtained wood framework a white color (inserts in Fig. 6b & c). Since RCD and YCD were added to wood skeleton, the agglomerates of CQDs could be clearly observed in the macropores (Fig. 6d & e). From N, P and S EDX mappings (Fig. 6d1 & e1) and spectra (Fig. 6d2 & e2) of lignin-retained woods loading with RCD and YCD, it could be confirmed that the CQDs have been assuredly and uniformly attached to the wood cell walls, which was consistent with the conclusion inferred in previous chapter and Fig. 5, even though the loading amounts of RCD and YCD were relatively low. Small amount of Na in YCD-LW and RCD-LW suggested that the lignin-retained wood obtained via the treatment with NaOH (3 wt%) and Na2EDTA (0.1 wt%) solution was not completely washed clean.

Furthermore, as seen in Fig. 6f, the lumens and cell walls of lignin-retained wood (wood framework) have been completely filled with EP, which effectively inhibited the light scattering of as-prepared transparent wood. From the physical diagram inserted in Fig. 6f, the LW-TW was so transparent that the pattern and text could be clearly seen through it. According to Fig. 6g & h, the lumens and cell walls of RTW and YTW have been occupied by EP, while their cell wall structure still can be observed in their magnified SEM images (Fig. 6g1 & h1). Apparently, the addition of two kinds of CQDs (RCD and YCD) did not affect the EP filling in lignin-retained wood. In addition, the physical picture inserted in Fig. 6g & h showed that the pattern and text under the RTW and YTW could be clearly seen. Thanks to the successful loading of RCD and YCD, the transparent wood under ultraviolet light can display corresponding purple and yellow fluorescence as provided in the inserts of Fig. 6g1 & h1. Notably, the substrate with text of “BHU” (without fluorescence) under both types of luminescent transparent woods could be easily recognized under visible light, even after irradiation of ultraviolet light. In conclusion, both CQDs (RCD and YCD) and EP have been full filled in and well combined with the LW uniformly and smoothly.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Optical property of luminescent transparent wood
ce:section.ce:para: Since the refractive index of EP is close to the wood formwork, the overall refractive index of transparent wood (containing LW-TW and DW-TW) was unified. Fig. 7a showed the transmittance spectra of two types of transparent woods. When the light shone on the transparent wood, the scattering between the internal resin and the wood formwork reduced, and the light could traverse transparent wood smoothly [9]. After the calculation according to Formula (S4–S6), the transmittances of LW-TW and DW-TW in the visible region of 400–800 nm were 79.7 % and 78.3 %, respectively, which were quite similar. Due to the more retention of lignin, the UV-B and UV-A light traversed LW-TW (41.4 %/38.7 %) was less than DW-TW (52.3 %/52.3 %), which was consistent with the results of lignin-retained wood (LW) and delignified wood (DW) presented in Fig. S1c and Table S1 (see Supplementary material). It indicated that the ultraviolet blocking capability of LW-TW was inherited from the LW due to its retaining lignin. Compared with LW-TW, the transmittance of YTW and RTW in ultraviolet band largely reduced, suggesting that the UV blocking performance of LW-TW indeed enhanced after the introducing YCD and RCD greatly. In the visible region of 400–800 nm, the transmittances of YTW and RTW reduced. This might be because CQDs (YCD and RCD) in YTW and RTW also had a certain absorption of visible light. After the calculation, the transmittances of RTW and YTW in the visible region were 69.7 % and 70.3 %, respectively. Due to the partial retention of lignin and ultraviolet absorption by CQDs, YTW was able to block 69.8 % of UV-B transmission and 73.9 % of UV-A transmission, meantime, RTW was able to block 78.8 % of UV-B transmission and 78 % of UV-A transmission.

Combined with the results from ultraviolet indicator card and irradiance meter recorded in Fig. 7b, the UV365nm light (30 mW/cm2) after traversing EP left a deep purple trace on the ultraviolet indicator card, and its UV365nm blocking rate was only 10.91 %. Then the UV365nm blocking rates of DW-TW, LW-TW, YTW and RTW were 59.92 %, 70.36 %, 80.49 % and 88.87 %, respectively. Especially, the UV365nm light after traversing RTW left only a very shallow trace on the ultraviolet indicator card, which could qualify the UV blocking requirements in various applications very well. Then the light scattering features of EP, DW-TW, LW-TW, YTW and RTW have been investigated as shown in Fig. 7c. Then the samples were illuminated with a laser spot with a diameter of approximately 500 mm successively, and the light scattering of glass and EP existed definitely. Due to the anisotropic microstructure and tightly stacked cellulose fibrils of wood framework, the refractive index of LW-TW changed slightly in the y direction, but varied greatly in the x direction, fully indicating its excellent anisotropic feature of optical property, which became the forming reason of the haze of transparent wood. The addition of YCD and RCD made the refractions of RTW and YTW more uniform, which could avoid the strong glare and qualify the demands as lighting window [43].

Stress-strain curves in Fig. 8 showed the anisotropic mechanical properties of wood samples in the directions parallel (y direction) and perpendicular (x direction) to the wood fiber. According to Fig. 8a1, the tensile strength and elongation at break of natural wood (NW) in the y direction were 2.78 MPa and 1.45 %, respectively. In contrast, the tensile strength and elongation at break of DW decreased significantly to 1 MPa and 0.8 %, respectively. Since the lignin serving as the adhesion agent could closely combine with cellulose to prevent its disintegration [44], LW retained more lignin displayed the better mechanical properties than delignified wood, the mechanical strength and elongation at break of which were 1.5 MPa and 1.42 %, respectively. In the x direction, natural wood, lignin-retained wood and delignified wood also showed the similar variation trend (Fig. 8b1). However, their tensile strengths weakened and elongations at break enhanced. Specifically, their tensile strengths decreased to 0.59 MPa, 0.39 MPa and 0.24 MPa, respectively, while their elongations at break increased to 5.14 %, 4.69 % and 3.78 %, respectively. EP with the matching refractive index tightly bind the cell wall and pipe hole inside the wood, which not only reduced the light refraction through the sample for achieving the transparent wood, but also significantly improved its mechanical properties [45]. Fig. 8a & b showed that the tensile strength and elongation at break of cured EP in the any directions were the same, which were 14.55 MPa and 2.1 %, respectively.

Compared with natural wood and EP, the mechanical properties of the transparent wood (LW-TW and DW-TW) were significantly improved. In the y direction, the tensile strengths of LW-TW and DW-TW were 16.25 MPa and 15.74 MPa, respectively, meanwhile, their elongations at break were 2.86 % and 2.85 %, respectively. In the x direction, their tensile strengths were 17.08 MPa and 16.99 MPa, respectively, while their elongations at break were 4.97 % and 3.95 %, respectively. Such mechanical enhancement was attributed to the formation of chemical bond between the hydroxyl group from wood and the epoxy group from EP [46], resulting in the strength improvement of interface between wood framework and EP. After encapsulation of CQDs (YCD and RCD), the mechanical properties of as-obtained YTW and RTW in y direction were significantly improved. Specifically, the tensile strength and elongation at break of YTW increased to 18.01 MPa and 2.98 %, respectively, which were 6.48 and 2.06 times of natural wood, respectively. Meantime, the tensile strength and elongation at break of RTW increased to 18.83 MPa and 3.12 %, respectively, which were 6.77 and 2.15 times of natural wood, respectively. In the x direction, YTW and RTW inherited the excellent extensibility of natural wood, and their tensile strengths were significantly improved. Specifically, the tensile strength and elongation at break of YTW increased to 16.91 MPa (28.66 times of natural wood) and 5.24 %, respectively, while the tensile strength and elongation at break of RTW increased to 19.39 MPa (32.86 times of natural wood) and 5.35 %, respectively. Compared with LW-TW, YTW and RTW significantly improved as well. This might be because the additions of YCD and RCD could improve the interface bonding between the cellulose and EP for further enhancing the mechanical strengths of YTW and RTW [47].

To further evaluate the potential application of YTW and RTW, their durability performances were investigated as well. Fig. 8c & d recorded the change of UV blocking rate of YTW and RTW after immersing in different solvents within 5 days. The results showed that their transparency and fluorescence had no variation. After the immersing treatment with acid solution (pH = 1) and alkali solution (pH = 14) for 5 days, the change of UV blocking rate of YTW and RTW reduced slightly, which were 99.09 %/99.13 % and 99.53 %/99.68 %, respectively. Specifically, the acid/alkali resistance time of YTW and RTW was quite long, a relatively great decline happened until the 3rd day. However, their UV blocking rates still maintained above the 99 % of their original performance. This is mainly attributed to the polymer structure of EP, which full filled the surface and internal of wood framework. EP molecule contains a large number of oxygen, carbon, hydrogen and other atoms, which constitute a stable chemical structure (3D network formed with epoxy groups and curing agent) with high crosslinking density.

Moreover, the hydroxyl, carboxyl and other functional groups on CQDs could form salts or esters by reacting with acid, alkali, etc., thereby neutralizing or stabilizing the acid/alkali solution. Besides, such functional groups would also form hydroxyl group, oxidized carbonyl group and other adsorption groups, which could adsorb and remove acid ion, alkali ion or other organic substance in water, thereby reducing the ion concentration and corrosiveness of surrounding acid/alkali solution. Due to the uniform distribution of hydroxyl, carboxyl and other functional groups as well as the stable chemical bonds, the CQDs had better stability and corrosion resistance [48]. In addition, the UV blocking rates of YTW and RTW after immersing in water, absolute ethanol and acetone hardly changed within 5 days, the transparency and fluorescence of which had no obvious variation. As presented in Fig. 8c & d, the text and pattern under the YTW and RTW after above evaluation still could be seen clearly.

Fig. 9a showed the model of transparent wood samples serving as the lighting window on the insulated chamber, which uses a digital thermometer to record the temperature variations inside. As shown in Fig. 9c, the heat in unblocked insulated chamber (wrapped by the aluminum foil with low heat conductivity) under illumination with one solar intensity could be transferred by the air through the unblocked area only. The free flow of air inside and outside the insulated chamber certainly might lead to the heat loss. Within 80 min, the temperature inside the unblocked insulated chamber slowly raised to 31.5 °C, while the temperature inside the insulated chamber with EP as the lighting window raised to 32.8 °C. This is because EP blocked the heat transfer through the air flow, leading to the higher temperature inside the insulated chamber [49]. LW-TW made of EP and wood framework (lignin-retained wood) inherited their merits, thus the temperature inside the insulated chamber raised to 33.3 °C. After the encapsulation of CQDs, the temperatures inside the insulated chambers with YTW and RTW as the lighting windows raised to 33.5 °C and 33.8 °C, respectively. Such faster and higher rising temperature might be attributed to the light absorption and photothermal conversion of YCD and RCD.

As shown from the cooling curves in Fig. 9d, the temperatures inside the unblocked insulated chamber and insulated chamber with EP spent 54 min and 85 min on decreasing to 21.2 °C, respectively. In addition, the temperatures inside the insulated chambers with LW-TW, YTW and RTW as the lighting windows respectively reduced to 22.1 °C, 21.9 °C and 22.4 °C within 90 min, suggesting the better insulation of as-obtained luminescent transparent wood, especially for RTW. This might be because luminescent LW-TW provided resistance for heat transfer in the fiber microstructure of wood framework, as well as the heat release from the addition of CQDs due to the photothermal effect, thus delaying heat flow and loss [50]. As presented in Fig. 9b, we measured the temperatures of EP, LW-TW, YTW and RTW during the illumination with one solar intensity (1 kW·m−2, 2 h). The surface temperatures of above four samples after irradiation could reach up to 37.8 °C, 38.5 °C, 39.1 °C and 39.4 °C, respectively. Apparently, the temperatures of YTW and RTW increased higher and faster than EP and LW-TW during the entire illumination process, which corresponded to the experiment results when they were serving as the lighting windows for the hotbox. Such phenomenon well explained that the rapid temperature rise of hotbox was indeed related to the increasing speed of sample temperature, the high temperature of which would influence on the temperature variation of internal air significantly. It could be predicted that our YTW and RTW as the lighting windows in cold areas would increase their temperatures under the solar irradiation and transfer the heat to the internal air of buildings, which could heat up the indoor temperatures rapidly. After losing the solar irradiation, YTW and RTW could maintain the high temperature for longer time, which certainly would be used as the ideal insulation materials.

In order to determine the degradation and residual weight of product at different temperatures, a thermogravimetric analyzer was used to performed the TG and DTG analysis of NW, LW, EP, LW-TW, YTW and RTW as presented in Fig. 10a & b. In the nitrogen atmosphere, the degradation of natural wood (NW) mainly experienced two stages. During the stage of 200–350 °C, about 50 % of its weight was lost in this stage. This is because the pyrolysis of hemicellulose began first in the temperature range of 180–350 °C, followed by the degradation of cellulose in the temperature range of 270–350 °C [51]. The next stage consisted of the pyrolysis of lignin with better thermal stability, and the further degradation of coke generated in the first stage. Notably, the partial lignin removal of LW might influence its pyrolysis. Besides, most hemicellulose was degraded during the lignin removal process as well [52]. Therefore, the lignin-retained wood (LW) started to degrade early, and displayed a faster degradation. EP degraded gradually in the temperature range of 320–500 °C. LW-TW, YTW and RTW showed the similar degradation pattern to EP. However, the wood framework (lignin-retained wood) containing the residual organic and volatile components resulted in their earlier beginning of degradation, and slowed down the entire degradation rates of LW-TW, YTW and RTW. This is because the EP covering the lignin-retained wood surface and filling in its pores efficiently impeded the degradation of wood components, thus greatly improving the thermal stability of LW-TW, YTW and RTW.

In the practical application scenarios, the wettability of wood materials is one of the most important factors must be considered. As shown in Fig. 10c, the water droplets were gradually absorbed by natural wood (NW) within 25 s due to the abundant micro/nano-pores structure and the consequent capillary absorption. After pretreatment, the hydrophilic cellulose in lignin-retained wood (LW) and delignified wood (DW) was exposed, thus they could absorb the water droplets more easily and rapidly. Generally, EP is hydrophobic due to its chemical structure containing a large number of nonpolar CC bonds and epoxy groups, which display the low affinity towards water. EP is usually obtained by the reaction between the amides, amines, acids and other compounds containing hydrophobic groups, and the phenols, alcohols, amines and other compounds containing epoxy groups. Such nonpolar groups of these compounds contribute to the hydrophobic property of EP [53]. The EP (E51) used in this work has a static water contact angle (WCA) of 63°, while the wood framework (LW or DW) impregnated with EP exhibits a similar WCA. As presented in Fig. 10d, WCAs of LW-TW and DW-TW were 65° and 64°, respectively, which have inherited the wettability of EP well. This is because the internal space of wood framework has been full filled with EP, which further formed a compact layer on the its surface as well. Notably, YTW and RTW inherited the wettability of LW-TW, the WCA of which were 69° and 65°, respectively. Certainly, their WCAs were also close to the EP used in this work, which was attributed to the EP/CQDs mixture filled inside and covered on surface of the lignin-retained wood. Compared to the hydrophilic natural wood and superhydrophilic lignin-retained wood with good water absorption, the as-prepared YTW and RTW allowed the water droplets stay their surfaces stably. This work certainly offered a better strategy for the surface cleaning and interior protection of luminescent transparent wood, concurrently satisfying the various requirements in different water-containing applications.

Functional modification in the process of preparing transparent wood could largely expand its functions such as fluorescence, ultraviolet blocking, heat preservation and insulation. In order to make better use of transparent wood, we adopted two types of as-prepared CQDs (yellow and red fluorescence) to further prepare the luminescent transparent woods. Specifically, two types of CQDs were prepared, meantime, two types of wood frameworks (DW and LW) were obtained. The wood frameworks were then combined with epoxy resin for achieving DW-TW and LW-TW. Results showed LW retained the more lignin (80.5 %) than DW, and LW-TW could block 58.6 % of UV-B light and 61.3 % of UV-A light, displaying a visible transmission of 79.7 %, which performed better than DW-TW. Then yellow and red luminescent transparent woods were prepared after the addition of CQDs to the LW-TW. The tensile strength and elongation at break of YTW reached up to 18.01 MP and 5.24 %, respectively, which could efficiently block the ultraviolet light (UV-B ~ 69.8 % and UV-A ~ 73.9 %), displaying the visible transmission of 69.7 % and UV blocking rate of 80.49 %. The tensile strength and elongation at break of RTW reached up to 19.39 MPa and 5.35 %, respectively, which could efficiently block the ultraviolet light (UV-B ~ 78.8 % and UV-A ~ 78 %), possessing the visible transmission of 70.3 % and UV blocking rate of 88.87 %. Notably, both transparent woods with yellow and red luminescence also exhibited the excellent water resistance, actual durability, thermal stability and insulation. So far, the luminescent transparent woods are still facing the limitation and challenge of hard large-scaled production. We believe in the near future, the luminescent transparent woods would be certain to enhance the adaptability, serviceability and large-scaled production of wood, and broaden its range of applications including lighting setup, sensor, green decoration, smart window, encryption and anti-counterfeiting, ultraviolet conversion and other fields.
Not applicable.
